exercises.name: The Stakes Opener
exercises.rule: Name what was at stake in your first two sentences.
exercises.why: A story only grips when the listener knows what could be lost.
exercises.objective: Train opening a personal story by making what could be lost, gained, or changed clear before any events unfold, so the listener has a reason to care from the first beat.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if what was at stake is not clear within the first two sentences, thinking_quality score MUST reflect the missing stakes.
exercises.coach_insight: Great openers name a concrete loss — a job, a friendship, a deadline someone was counting on — not just that it 'mattered.' The trap is describing the situation's importance instead of what would break if things went wrong.
common_failure_modes: Opens with scene-setting or backstory, so the stakes only surface after the events are underway
Labels importance ('it was a big deal', 'it really mattered') without naming what could actually be lost or gained
Names a stake early but abandons it — the events that follow never answer to that stake
Piles up several competing stakes so the listener can't tell which one the story is about
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Beyond the two-sentence constraint, weight how concrete the named stake is and whether the rest of the story keeps that stake alive and pays it off.
exercises.retry_objective: Make the stakes explicit within the first 20 seconds, then let every later event answer to those stakes.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true first-person story with a named listener and a reason they are hearing it, told in 60-90 seconds. Scenarios vary across work, school, and life, but every prompt must contain something that could genuinely be lost or gained so stakes are available early. The prompt never tells the user to state stakes first — that is the exercise rule.

prompts.text: You're in a job interview. Tell a 90-second story about a time you took on something you weren't sure you could handle, and make clear what was riding on it.
A new teammate asks what it's really like to work with you. Tell a short story about a week that tested you, making clear what could have gone wrong.
You're catching up with an old friend. Tell the story of a decision you agonized over, making clear what you stood to lose either way.
You're speaking to interns on their first day. Tell a story about an early mistake you nearly made, making clear what was at risk before you say how it ended.
A mentor asks why you chose your current path. Tell a story about the moment the choice got real, making clear what saying no would have cost you.
You're on a panel for students entering your field. Tell a story about a deadline that mattered, making clear who was counting on you and what failure would have meant.
At a team retro, share a story about a call you made under pressure, making clear what hung on getting it right before you reveal the outcome.
You're recording a short intro for a podcast. Tell the story of a risk you took that others advised against, making clear what you were putting on the line.
A friend is about to make a leap you once made. Tell them the story of your version, making clear what you feared losing at the time.
You're welcoming a new hire over coffee. Tell a story about a project that could have damaged your reputation, making the stakes clear before the events.
You have 90 seconds at an all-hands to explain why you push so hard on quality. Tell one story where the cost of sloppiness got personal, stakes first.
You're closing a workshop you led. Tell a story about the moment you nearly quit something that mattered, making the audience feel what quitting would have cost.

exercises.name: The Honest Wreck
exercises.rule: Own the failure plainly. End with what changed.
exercises.why: Owning a failure without excuses is what makes the lesson believable.
exercises.objective: Train telling a failure story that names the speaker's own misjudgment without blame-shifting or softening, and closes on the concrete change it produced.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response shifts blame, hides the speaker's own part, or ends without a concrete change, thinking_quality score MUST reflect the dodge.
exercises.coach_insight: The strongest version has one plain sentence — 'I misjudged X' — and a change specific enough to picture: a habit, a checklist, a question you now ask first. The trap is packaging the failure so it secretly flatters you.
common_failure_modes: Attributes the failure to circumstances, timing, or other people instead of naming their own misjudgment
Softens ownership with hedges ('to be fair', 'in hindsight the context was tough', 'mistakes were made')
Ends on a vague resolution ('I learned a lot', 'I grew from it') instead of a concrete changed behavior
Spends the close rehabilitating their image or re-litigating the situation rather than naming what changed
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the plainness of the ownership sentence and the specificity of the closing change over the polish or drama of the narrative itself.
exercises.retry_objective: State your own misjudgment in one plain sentence and make the closing change specific enough to picture.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true failure story with a named listener and a reason the failure is worth hearing, told in 60-120 seconds. Contexts vary across interviews, mentoring, retros, and talks; every prompt must involve a failure the speaker genuinely owned so honesty, not performance, carries the story. The prompt never says 'don't make excuses' — resisting the dodge is the exercise.

prompts.text: An interviewer asks about a time you failed. Tell a 90-second story about a real mistake, owning your part and ending with what you do differently now.
Your mentee just made their first big mistake. Tell them the story of one of yours, honest about your part in it and what it changed in you.
Over dinner, a friend asks about your worst week at work. Tell the story straight — what you got wrong, and what you took from it.
At a team retro, share a story about a decision of yours that backfired, naming your own misjudgment and the habit you built afterward.
You're speaking to new grads at an orientation. Tell a story about a failure early in your path, honest about the cause and clear about what changed.
A junior colleague is beating themselves up over an error. Tell the story of a time you dropped something important, and what owning it taught you.
You're giving a five-minute failure talk at a team offsite. Pick one real wreck, name your part in it plainly, and end with what changed.
A podcast host asks for the mistake that taught you the most. Tell it without softening your role, and finish with the change it forced.
Your new manager asks in a 1:1 about a project that went sideways. Tell the story, own the misjudgment, and say what you'd do differently today.
You're on a panel about lessons from failure. Tell one story where the fault was genuinely yours, and make the after clearly different from the before.
At an all-hands, leadership asks teams to share honest misses. Tell the story of a call you defended too long, and what finally changed your mind.
You're mentoring a founder whose launch just flopped. Tell the story of your own flop — what you misread, what it cost, and the practice you kept from it.

exercises.name: The Hinge Moment
exercises.rule: Build to one turning point. Show before and after.
exercises.why: A story lands when the listener can feel the exact moment everything turned.
exercises.objective: Train shaping a story around a single turning point — tension building before it, a clearly different world after it — instead of narrating events at a flat, even pace.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the story lacks one identifiable turning point with a distinct before and after, structure score MUST reflect the flat or multi-hinged arc.
exercises.coach_insight: Spend most of your time before the turn — let pressure stack until the moment has to break, then show a world that's visibly different. The trap is narrating events at equal weight, which turns a hinge into a timeline nobody can feel.
common_failure_modes: Gives away the turn or outcome in the opening, leaving nothing for the middle to build toward
Narrates events at flat, even weight so no single moment stands out as the hinge
Offers two or three turning points instead of committing to one
Describes the 'after' in the same terms as the 'before', so nothing visibly changed
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight how tension escalates toward the turn and how sharply the after contrasts with the before, rather than the completeness of the events recounted.
exercises.retry_objective: Hold the turn until the tension peaks, then make life after the hinge visibly different from life before it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true first-person story about a change in direction, told to a named listener in 60-120 seconds. Topics vary across career, projects, beliefs, and relationships to work; every prompt must contain a real pivot so a single hinge is available. The prompt never labels the turning point — finding and staging it is the exercise.

prompts.text: Introduce yourself to a new team by telling the story of the moment you decided to do the work you do now. Make life before and after that moment distinct.
A friend asks how you ended up where you are. Tell the story of one conversation that changed your direction, building to it rather than starting with it.
You're at a dinner with old classmates. Tell the story of a small decision that turned out to be a big one, letting the turn land in the middle, not the start.
In a job interview, tell the story of a project that changed how you work. Build the tension before the shift, then show what's different since.
You're speaking to students choosing their field. Tell the story of the moment you realized your first plan was wrong, and what pivoting opened up.
At a team offsite, tell the story of a piece of feedback that stung and then changed you. Let the audience feel the before, the sting, and the after.
A mentee asks about a time something finally made sense after a long struggle. Tell the story of that hinge — the pressure building up to it and the change that followed.
You're introducing yourself to a group that shares an interest of yours. Tell the story of how that interest deepened, building to the moment it stopped being casual.
Over coffee, a colleague asks why you walked away from something you had invested a lot in. Tell the story so the turning point lands as a moment, not a summary.
You're opening a workshop with a personal story. Pick a day that split your work into before and after, and make the audience feel the hinge.
At an alumni event, you have two minutes. Tell the story of the failure or encounter that redirected your path, holding the turn until the tension peaks.
A documentary crew asks for the moment a group effort you were part of changed direction. Tell it so the turn arrives once, clearly, and the ending shows a changed world.

exercises.name: Where It Began
exercises.rule: Tell it like you lived it, not like a resume.
exercises.why: Listeners connect to the feeling of an origin, not the facts of one.
exercises.objective: Train telling an origin story in a voice that sounds genuinely lived-in — warm, specific, and emotionally present — instead of a recited biography.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the delivery sounds recited, flat, or bio-like rather than emotionally present and warm, tone score MUST reflect the missing authenticity.
exercises.coach_insight: Find the one detail you can still see — the room, the sound, the exact sentence someone said — and slow down when you reach it. The trap is walking through your history in order, which sounds like a resume even when the facts are moving.
common_failure_modes: Recites a chronological career summary — dates, roles, milestones — instead of inhabiting a lived moment
Stays in generic abstractions ('I've always been passionate about...') with no sensory or specific detail
Holds one flat register throughout, with no shift in energy or word choice at the moment that matters
Performs enthusiasm through superlatives ('amazing', 'incredible') rather than showing it through a specific memory
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the presence of felt, specific detail and audible shifts in engagement over narrative structure or the completeness of the origin timeline.
exercises.retry_objective: Pick the one detail that still means something to you and let your voice change when you reach it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask how something in the speaker's real life began — a craft, a cause, a career, a team — told to a named listener in 60-90 seconds. Settings vary from casual to formal; every prompt must invite sensory or emotional detail so authenticity is available. The prompt never instructs a tone — sounding lived-in rather than recited is the exercise.

prompts.text: Your new team asks how you got into this work. Tell the origin story like you're remembering it, with the detail that still sticks with you.
At a meetup, someone asks how you got into something you've spent years getting better at. Tell the story of where it started, sounding like yourself, not a bio.
A friend's kid is curious about your job. Tell them the story of the first time it hooked you, warm and unpolished.
You're opening a talk with your origin story. Tell the room about the moment this field chose you, letting them hear that it still matters to you.
A new coworker asks why you hold the standards you do. Tell the story of the frustration that set them, sounding invested, not scripted.
In a podcast interview, the host asks for your how-it-started story. Tell it with the texture of the real moment — where you were, what you noticed.
You're meeting a group working on an issue you care about. Tell the story of the first time that issue felt personal to you, letting the feeling reach your voice.
A mentee asks if you always knew what you wanted. Tell the true, wandering version of your origin, comfortable with the detours.
At a family gathering, a relative finally asks what you actually do. Tell them the story of how it began, like a story, not a job description.
You're introducing yourself at a leadership program. Tell the story of the person or moment that set your path, letting warmth carry the telling.
You're giving a short toast at a school reunion. Tell the story of something your school years started in you, and let the room feel it.
In a final-round interview, you're asked what really drives you. Answer with the story of a moment when that drive first showed up — honest, not a pitch.

exercises.name: Land the Lesson
exercises.rule: One sentence of lesson at the end. Then stop.
exercises.why: A story earns exactly one takeaway — trailing extras dilute the one that matters.
exercises.objective: Train closing a lesson-learned story with a single earned takeaway sentence — no second morals, no trailing qualifiers — so the story ends where its meaning peaks.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the story does not end on exactly one takeaway sentence, or keeps talking after it, structure score MUST reflect the diluted close.
exercises.coach_insight: Everything in the story should quietly point at the closing line, so when it arrives it feels inevitable. The trap is not trusting it — adding a qualifier or second moral that tells the listener the first ending wasn't enough.
common_failure_modes: Keeps talking after the takeaway — trailing qualifiers, a second moral, or an 'anyway...' fade-out
Delivers the lesson as several sentences or a list of learnings instead of one line
Ends on a lesson the story as told doesn't actually support
States the moral up front, so the ending is a repeat rather than a landing
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the final stretch of the response — whether the story's meaning peaks at, and stops on, the closing sentence — over the quality of the events that precede it.
exercises.retry_objective: Cut every moral but one, sharpen it to a single sentence, and let it be the last thing you say.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true story that taught the speaker something, told to a named listener who could use the lesson, in 60-90 seconds. Contexts vary across mentoring, teams, and public settings; every story must genuinely support a one-line takeaway. The prompt may ask for a lesson but never dictates the one-sentence close — the discipline of stopping is the exercise.

prompts.text: Tell a new intern the story behind a rule you never break at work, ending with the lesson in one clean sentence.
A friend asks for advice before their first big presentation. Tell the story of a time you had to perform under pressure and it went sideways, and land one lesson at the end.
At a book club, share a story about advice you ignored and later understood. Close with the lesson in a single sentence, then stop.
In a mentoring session, tell the story of the hardest feedback you ever received, ending on one sentence you'd want your mentee to keep.
You're closing a team retro. Tell the story of the roughest moment from a shared effort you know firsthand, and end with the one lesson the group should carry forward.
An interviewer asks what experience shaped you most. Tell that story and finish with one sentence of takeaway, no trailing qualifiers.
You're speaking at a training for new team leads. Tell the story of a time you were trusted with responsibility and misjudged something, ending with the single lesson it burned in.
A colleague is about to repeat a mistake you once made. Tell them your version of the story and end on the one line you wish someone had told you.
On a panel about career growth, tell the story of a bet on yourself that paid off or didn't, closing with exactly one lesson.
You're recording a two-minute video for your company's learning library. Tell one story from a real project and end with its single takeaway.
At an all-hands after a difficult stretch, tell the story of a setback you lived through, and give the room one sentence to remember, nothing after it.
You're giving a short address to graduating students. Tell one story from your path and land one earned lesson — not three, not a montage.

exercises.name: Two Sides, One Scene
exercises.rule: Make the other side's case as concrete as yours.
exercises.why: A conflict story is only credible when both positions feel real and specific.
exercises.objective: Train telling a conflict story where both positions are rendered in concrete, specific terms — what each side wanted and why it made sense to them — so tension comes from substance, not caricature.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if either side's position is vague, strawmanned, or stated only in abstractions, clarity score MUST reflect the one-sided or fuzzy telling.
exercises.coach_insight: Give the other side the strongest version of their case — their actual constraint, number, or deadline — so a stranger could argue it for them. The trap is granting your own position the vivid detail while theirs gets a one-line summary.
common_failure_modes: Renders their own position in specifics while the counterpart's stays a vague summary
Strawmans the other side ('they just didn't get it', 'they wanted to be difficult') instead of stating a defensible case
Keeps both positions abstract — 'we had different priorities' — with no names, numbers, or constraints
Jumps to the resolution before the substance of the two positions has been established
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the balance of specificity between the two positions — especially how fairly the counterpart's case is built — over how elegantly the conflict resolves.
exercises.retry_objective: Rebuild the other side's position with one specific fact or constraint per claim until a stranger could argue it convincingly.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true story of a real disagreement, told to a named listener with a reason to hear it, in 60-120 seconds. Conflicts vary across colleagues, teams, family, and leadership; every prompt must involve a counterpart with a genuinely defensible position so concreteness on both sides is possible. The prompt asks for fairness in plain words but never scripts how to achieve it.

prompts.text: Tell a friend about a disagreement with a coworker where you both had a point. Make each side's position concrete before you say how it resolved.
In a job interview, describe a conflict on a past team. Name what each person specifically wanted and why it made sense to them.
At dinner, tell the story of a family debate that got heated. Give the other side's best argument as vividly as your own.
Your new manager asks how you handle conflict. Tell one real story, grounding both positions in specifics — dates, numbers, exact concerns.
At a team workshop on collaboration, tell the story of a standoff between two groups you saw up close, making each side's pressure concrete.
A mentee is stuck in a feud with a peer. Tell the story of a similar clash of yours, stating each side's stakes so plainly they could argue either one.
On a panel about difficult colleagues, tell a story where the difficult person turned out to have a real point. Make their case in specifics.
A new member of a group you're in asks about its old tensions. Tell the story of a disagreement between two people you knew well, both positions in concrete terms.
An interviewer asks about a time you disagreed with someone in charge. Tell the story with that person's actual constraints as clear as your own reasoning.
At a retro, recount the sharpest debate from a project you were part of. Reconstruct both arguments with enough detail that the room can weigh them.
In a mediation training, share a real conflict where you misjudged the other side. Rebuild their position in concrete detail before telling how it ended.
In a leadership program, tell the story of a conflict where both sides were right about different things. Make each truth specific before the resolution.

exercises.name: The Two-Sentence Runway
exercises.rule: Two sentences of setup, then the story must move.
exercises.why: A story loses its listener in the backstory it didn't need.
exercises.objective: Train opening a story with only the context the listener actually needs — two sentences of orientation, then events — instead of drowning the start in history or beginning so abruptly the listener can't follow.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if setup runs past two sentences before the first event, or the listener lacks the context to follow that event, conciseness score MUST reflect the imbalance.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick the two facts the story collapses without — usually who's involved and what they were trying to do — and save the rest for when the story needs it. The trap is believing the listener needs the history to care; they need motion.
common_failure_modes: Opens with a history lesson — three or more sentences of background before anything happens
Front-loads names, dates, and organizational detail the story never uses again
Starts so abruptly the listener can't tell who is involved or why the events matter
Stops the story mid-action to backfill context that belonged in the setup
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight what the speaker chose to leave out — whether every piece of included context earns its place in the story — not just the sentence count before the first event.
exercises.retry_objective: Pick the two facts the listener cannot follow the story without, say them, and let the first event arrive by sentence three.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true first-person story with a named listener and a rich, tempting backstory — long histories, tangled context — told in 60-90 seconds. Scenarios vary across work, school, and life; every prompt must invite a real experience carrying more background than the story needs so selection is required. The prompt never says to trim the setup — knowing what to cut is the exercise.

prompts.text: Your new coworker asks about the most tangled situation you've ever helped sort out. The full history behind it goes back a long way — tell them the story.
A friend asks about someone you were once close to but drifted away from. Years of history sit behind it. Tell the story.
Your cousin asks about the longest you've ever waited or worked for something you wanted. There's a whole saga behind it. Tell it.
A date asks about a day when your plans completely fell apart. The buildup to that day is a story in itself. Tell it.
In an interview, you're asked about a piece of work you put real effort into that never saw the light of day. The context behind it was tangled. Tell the story of what happened.
At a retro, recount a time a routine everyone trusted finally broke down. The quirks behind it have a long history.
A mentee asks how you got good at something that took you years. The backstory is long and winding — tell them the story of when it finally clicked.
Tell your team the story of a time something important nearly went out the door with a serious flaw. The chain of events behind it was a mess.
At a dinner party, someone asks about a gathering you attended where nothing went to plan. Half the story starts long before that day.
You have two minutes at an all-hands to tell the story of a time you helped rescue something that was close to failing. The history behind it runs deep.
On a podcast, the host asks about the closest you've ever come to giving up on something that mattered to you. Years of twists sit behind it.
You're briefing a new decision-maker on a disagreement that has dragged on for a long time. The history is long, and they need the story of what went wrong.

exercises.name: Make It Theirs
exercises.rule: End on an insight the listener can use without your story.
exercises.why: A personal story matters to a listener only when it becomes about them.
exercises.objective: Train landing a personal anecdote on a transferable insight — one the listener could apply to their own situation — so the story earns its telling instead of staying a private memory.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the story ends without a transferable insight the listener could apply to their own situation, or the insight doesn't follow from the story told, thinking_quality score MUST reflect the missing bridge.
exercises.coach_insight: Before you speak, decide what your story proves for someone who wasn't there, and say the ending in their terms — their fear, their next step. The trap is closing on what it meant to you, which leaves the listener holding your souvenir.
common_failure_modes: Ends inside the memory — the story finishes but never turns toward the listener's situation
Closes with a self-referential lesson ('so I learned...') instead of one framed for the listener to use
Reaches for a generic platitude ('never give up') that any story could have produced
Offers an insight that doesn't actually follow from the events just told
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the final bridge — whether the closing insight is earned by this specific story yet stated so the listener can act on it — over the vividness of the anecdote itself.
exercises.retry_objective: Ask what your story proves for someone who wasn't there, and end on that — stated for their life, not yours.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a true personal story told to a named listener whose situation differs from the speaker's, in 60-120 seconds. Listeners vary across mentees, teams, friends, and audiences; every prompt must give the listener a live concern so a bridge from the speaker's experience to the listener's world is available. The prompt never asks for the broader point — building that bridge is the exercise.

prompts.text: Someone close to you is dreading their first day at a new job. Tell them a story from your own first weeks somewhere.
A friend's project just got rejected. Tell them about a time something you made got turned down.
A new grad asks if it's normal to feel lost at work. Tell a story about a stretch when you had no idea what you were doing.
Your mentee bombed a presentation this morning. Tell them the story of a time you stumbled in front of other people.
At a team offsite icebreaker, share a story from outside work that shaped how you work.
An interviewer asks what you do outside of work and why it matters. Tell a story from something you do purely because you choose to.
You're mentoring a founder who's afraid to raise prices. Tell a story from your own life about asking for more than felt comfortable.
A student asks why you keep showing up for a commitment nobody pays you for. Tell them one story from a single day of it.
Your team is flat after losing a big deal. Open Monday's meeting with a story from your own career.
You're giving a five-minute talk at a conference far outside your field. All you have is one personal story the audience didn't come to hear.
You're closing an all-hands during a rough quarter. Your material: one story from the least glamorous work you've ever done.
On a leadership panel, you're asked what shaped your management style. The honest answer is a story from your childhood.
